title: One bench at a time
summary: A junior planner in a Tokyo ward office spends two years arguing for a single bench — and discovers what bureaucratic patience can and cannot achieve.
body:
Saori Ueda joined the urban planning section of a Tokyo ward office at twenty-six. Her first independent project was, by her own description, embarrassingly small: a single bench, near a bus stop on a slope, requested by elderly residents who could no longer wait standing up.

The bench took two years. Saori expected resistance from the budget department and was prepared for it. She had not expected resistance from the engineers, who pointed out that the pavement was 1.9 metres wide and a bench would reduce the clear walking width below the 1.6-metre guideline. She had not expected resistance from the local shopkeepers, who feared that a bench would attract people who did not buy anything. She had not expected resistance from the bus company, which had a separate plan for that stop and did not want a bench to fix the current location in place.

She wrote memos. She walked the slope at different hours of the day with a measuring tape. She found that, between 7 a.m. and 9 a.m., the pavement carried sixty-two pedestrians and four people who stood waiting for the bus, two of whom held a wall for support. She wrote this in a paper that she circulated to seven departments. She organised one meeting in which an eighty-one-year-old resident, a retired postwoman, described what it felt like to wait for the bus on that slope in winter rain.

The bench was installed on a Tuesday morning in March. It was the type Saori had specified: low, deep, with armrests that helped older people stand back up. The pavement was clear to 1.55 metres, fifty millimetres below the guideline, which her director had agreed to permit as an exception. The bus company eventually accepted a clause that the bench would be moved if the stop relocated. The shopkeepers were not consulted at the final stage; one of them complained for a month, then stopped.

Saori has placed six benches since then, in three years. She has learned that the slowness is not, as she once thought, a sign that the system does not care. The slowness is what protects benches from being removed quickly by the next person who has a different idea. She has decided, for now, to stay in this job. She is not sure she will recommend it to her younger colleagues. She suspects most of them imagine planning as drawing a new neighbourhood, not as arguing for fifty millimetres of pavement for two years.

annotations.meaningVi: văn phòng phường (đơn vị hành chính ở Nhật)
chiều rộng đi bộ thông thoáng
hướng dẫn / tiêu chuẩn (không bắt buộc tuyệt đối)

annotations.phrase: fix the current location in place
annotations.meaningVi: cố định vị trí hiện tại (khó dời đi)
đã lưu hành (gửi qua nhiều bộ phận)
vẽ thiết kế một khu phố mới

grammarNotes.title: Repeated 'She had not expected' — past perfect for contrast
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Past perfect ('had + V3') trong bài lặp lại để nhấn mạnh các bất ngờ trước thời điểm tường thuật. 'Saori expected resistance from the budget department' (past simple — kỳ vọng đã có). 'She had not expected resistance from the engineers' (past perfect — sự bất ngờ trước thời điểm câu chuyện diễn ra). Cấu trúc này tạo nhịp ba lần ('She had not expected … She had not expected … She had not expected …') — kỹ thuật nhấn mạnh bằng song song hoá. Ở B1, bạn không cần tự viết kiểu này, nhưng cần nhận ra past perfect để hiểu thứ tự thời gian.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Past perfect ('had + V3') is repeated in the passage to mark surprises that pre-date the narrative moment. 'Saori expected resistance from the budget department' (past simple — expectation in place). 'She had not expected resistance from the engineers' (past perfect — the not-expecting was before the events unfolded). The triple parallel ('She had not expected …') is rhetorical emphasis. At B1 you do not need to write this yourself; you need to read past perfect and place events in order.

translationVi:
Saori Ueda gia nhập bộ phận quy hoạch đô thị của một văn phòng phường ở Tokyo năm hai mươi sáu tuổi. Dự án độc lập đầu tiên của cô, theo lời cô, nhỏ đến mức ngượng: một cái ghế băng duy nhất, gần một bến xe buýt trên dốc, do người già trong khu xin vì không còn đứng đợi nổi.

Cái ghế mất hai năm. Saori biết sẽ có phản đối từ phòng ngân sách và đã chuẩn bị. Cô không lường trước phản đối từ các kỹ sư, họ chỉ ra rằng vỉa hè rộng 1,9 mét và một cái ghế sẽ làm chiều rộng đi bộ thông thoáng xuống dưới 1,6 mét tiêu chuẩn. Cô không lường trước phản đối từ chủ cửa hàng địa phương, sợ ghế sẽ thu hút người không mua gì. Cô không lường trước phản đối từ công ty xe buýt, có kế hoạch riêng cho điểm dừng đó và không muốn ghế cố định vị trí hiện tại.

Cô viết các bản ghi nhớ. Cô đi bộ trên dốc nhiều giờ trong ngày với thước dây. Cô thấy giữa 7 và 9 giờ sáng, vỉa hè có sáu mươi hai người đi bộ và bốn người đứng đợi xe buýt, hai trong số đó vịn tường. Cô viết điều này trong một tài liệu gửi đến bảy phòng ban. Cô tổ chức một cuộc họp trong đó một cư dân tám mươi mốt tuổi, một nữ bưu tá đã nghỉ hưu, mô tả cảm giác đợi xe trên dốc đó vào mưa đông.

Ghế được lắp vào một sáng thứ Ba tháng Ba. Đúng loại Saori đã chỉ định: thấp, sâu, có tay vịn giúp người già đứng dậy. Vỉa hè còn thông thoáng 1,55 mét, dưới tiêu chuẩn năm mươi mi-li-mét, mà giám đốc đã đồng ý cho ngoại lệ. Công ty xe buýt cuối cùng chấp nhận điều khoản rằng ghế sẽ được dời nếu bến chuyển. Chủ cửa hàng không được hỏi ý kiến cuối cùng; một người phàn nàn một tháng rồi thôi.

Saori đã đặt sáu cái ghế từ đó, trong ba năm. Cô đã hiểu rằng sự chậm chạp, như cô từng nghĩ, không phải dấu hiệu hệ thống không quan tâm. Sự chậm chạp là cái bảo vệ những cái ghế khỏi bị người tiếp theo có ý kiến khác dỡ đi nhanh chóng. Cô quyết định, bây giờ, ở lại công việc này. Cô không chắc sẽ khuyên đồng nghiệp trẻ làm. Cô nghi hầu hết họ tưởng quy hoạch là vẽ một khu phố mới, không phải tranh luận năm mươi mi-li-mét vỉa hè trong hai năm.

questions.prompt: What was Saori's first independent project?
options: A single bench near a bus stop on a slope

questions.prompt: How long did the bench take to install?

options: A bench would reduce clear walking width below the 1.6-metre guideline

questions.prompt: Why did the bus company object?
options: It had a separate plan and did not want the bench to fix the current location in place

questions.prompt: What did Saori measure between 7 and 9 a.m.?
options: 62 pedestrians; 4 waiting for the bus, 2 holding a wall

questions.prompt: Who spoke at the meeting Saori organised?
options: An 81-year-old retired postwoman describing waiting in winter rain

questions.prompt: What kind of bench was installed?
options: Low, deep, with armrests to help older people stand up
A wooden bench with no back

questions.prompt: What was the final clear pavement width, and how was the exception handled?
options: 1.55 m, 50 mm below guideline; her director agreed to permit it as an exception

questions.prompt: What has Saori learned about slowness?
options: It is what protects benches from being removed quickly by the next person with a different idea

questions.prompt: What does she think her younger colleagues imagine planning is?
options: Drawing a new neighbourhood, not arguing for 50 mm of pavement for two years
The shopkeepers were happy from the start
The bus company eventually accepted a relocation clause
Saori has placed six benches since
Tiny pieces of public space require slow, multi-party negotiation, and that slowness — initially frustrating — turns out to be the protection of the result

The bench took two years. Saori expected resistance from the budget department and was prepared for it. She had not expected resistance from the engineers, who pointed out that the pavement was 1.9 metres wide and a bench would reduce the clear walking width below the {{b2}}-metre guideline. She wrote memos. She walked the slope at different hours of the day with a measuring {{b4}}. She organised one meeting in which an eighty-one-year-old resident, a retired {{b5}}, described what it felt like to wait for the bus on that slope in winter rain.

The pavement was clear to 1.55 metres, fifty millimetres below the guideline, which her director had agreed to permit as an {{b7}}. The shopkeepers were not consulted at the final stage; one of them complained for a month, then stopped.

Saori has placed six benches since then, in three years. She has learned that the slowness is not, as she once thought, a sign that the system does not care. The slowness is what protects benches from being {{b9}} quickly by the next person who has a different idea. She has decided, for now, to stay in this job. She is not sure she will recommend it to her younger colleagues. She suspects most of them imagine planning as {{b10}} a new neighbourhood, not as arguing for fifty millimetres of pavement for two years.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Saori suggests slowness is what protects results from being undone by the next person with a different idea. Pick a decision in your own community (a crossing, a sign, a rule) that you wish were made faster. What would be lost if it could be undone as quickly as it was made?
